description: Educational fixture/demo landing page about the Ralph Loop, built as a static HTML/CSS/JS site for dogfooding the NOOA Ralph runner without coupling to the main product.

userStories.title: Scaffold a static demo shell
userStories.description: As a reviewer of the Ralph loop, I want the demo to exist as an isolated static site fixture so the loop can implement and verify it without touching the main product.
acceptanceCriteria: The demo lives in an isolated fixture/demo directory, not in the main product surface.
The demo includes at minimum src/features/demo/index.html, src/features/demo/styles.css, and src/features/demo/script.js.
The page opens directly in a browser without requiring a build step or backend server.
The demo declares itself as an educational Ralph Loop fixture/demo.
userStories.notes: Keep the implementation HTML/CSS/JS only. No framework, no API, no package-level complexity unless strictly needed for local preview. For this story, do not run `nooa scaffold command`; create/update only the static files under src/features/demo/.

userStories.title: Explain the Ralph Loop in the hero and overview
userStories.description: As someone discovering Ralph for the first time, I want the page header and opening sections to explain what the Ralph Loop is and why it exists so I understand the premise in under a minute.
acceptanceCriteria: The hero defines Ralph Loop in plain language.
The opening copy explains the problem it solves: fresh context, peer review, CLI-first verification, and iterative story execution.
The page frames Ralph as an educational process runner, not generic AI hype.
The visual presentation feels intentional and editorial rather than template-like.
Implementation updates are applied to src/features/demo/index.html and, when needed, src/features/demo/styles.css or src/features/demo/script.js.
userStories.notes: Prefer concise and concrete copy. Avoid marketing fluff. The tone should teach, not sell.

userStories.title: Visualize the macro-loop and micro-loop
userStories.description: As a reader trying to operationalize the process, I want to see the difference between the feature-level macro-loop and the story-level micro-loop so I can understand how planning, review, TDD, and execution fit together.
acceptanceCriteria: The page contains a clear visual section for the macro-loop: plan, peer review rounds, approved backlog.
The page contains a clear visual section for the micro-loop: story selection, TDD, CLI/core-first implementation, verification, peer review, approval.
The relationship between the two loops is explained in prose alongside the visuals.
The visuals remain legible on mobile widths.
Implementation updates are applied to src/features/demo/index.html and, when needed, src/features/demo/styles.css or src/features/demo/script.js.
userStories.notes: Use static HTML/CSS/JS only. Lightweight animation is fine, but the meaning must remain available without motion.

userStories.title: Teach the key Ralph principles
userStories.description: As a developer evaluating the approach, I want dedicated sections for peer review, TDD, CLI-first verification, and learnings promotion so I understand the behavioral constraints that make the loop trustworthy.
acceptanceCriteria: The page explains why the worker cannot self-approve.
The page explains why TDD and CLI-first verification reduce theatrical code.
The page explains how learnings are captured locally first and only promoted when justified.
Each principle is explained with concrete wording rather than vague slogans.
Implementation updates are applied to src/features/demo/index.html and, when needed, src/features/demo/styles.css or src/features/demo/script.js.
userStories.notes: This section should feel educational and operational. It can include short command examples or structured callouts, but should remain static.

userStories.title: Polish the demo for real dogfooding
userStories.description: As someone using this page to validate the Ralph runner, I want the final demo to be polished enough to feel deliberate, responsive, and easy to inspect so it serves as a credible end-to-end fixture.
acceptanceCriteria: The page is responsive on desktop and mobile.
The page has accessible heading structure, readable contrast, and visible focus states for interactive elements.
JavaScript is limited to progressive enhancements such as navigation, section reveals, or diagram toggles.
A final section shows example Ralph-related commands or a CTA to inspect/run the loop locally.
Implementation updates are applied to src/features/demo/index.html and, when needed, src/features/demo/styles.css or src/features/demo/script.js.
userStories.notes: Favor restrained polish over complexity. The success condition is a believable educational demo that the Ralph loop can implement and verify.
